title: CSR: Surgery & Procedures (Scheduling & Client Care)
description: Everything a CSR handles around surgical and procedure days — scheduling, pre-op client instructions, estimates and consent, drop-off, and discharge coordination. No clinical surgery knowledge required.

lessons.title: Setting Up a Smooth Surgery Day

blocks.content:
Surgical days run on tight choreography, and the CSR sets them up. Your responsibilities typically include:

Scheduling: booking the procedure into the right surgical slot per clinic protocol, and coordinating drop-off times.

Pre-op instructions — this is critical: clients usually must follow fasting instructions (often no food after a certain time the night before, sometimes specific water rules). Conveying these clearly and confirming the client understands prevents canceled surgeries and safety issues.

Confirming details: drop-off time, what to bring, expected pick-up window.

Getting fasting instructions wrong can mean a surgery has to be canceled (a fasted patient is safer under anesthesia), so accuracy and clear confirmation here genuinely matter.
Always confirm the client understands pre-op fasting instructions — repeat them and have the client acknowledge. A pet that ate breakfast may have its surgery postponed for safety.

blocks.front: Why are pre-op fasting instructions so important for a CSR to convey clearly?
blocks.back: A fasted patient is safer under anesthesia; if instructions aren't followed, the surgery may have to be canceled or postponed.

blocks.prompt: A client booking a spay asks, 'Can she eat breakfast that morning?' What's the best response?

options.feedback: This contradicts standard fasting protocol and could force a cancellation or risk safety.

options.text: Convey your clinic's fasting instructions clearly (typically no food after a set time the night before) and confirm she understands
options.feedback: Correct — give the clinic's specific fasting guidance and confirm understanding.
Fasting matters significantly for anesthetic safety.

blocks.content:
Surgery involves important paperwork and a smooth hand-off:

Estimates: provide a clear written estimate for the procedure beforehand so there are no surprises (you covered estimates in your Payments course — surgery is where they matter most).

Consent forms: ensure consent is completed and signed, and that the client has provided current contact numbers so the team can reach them during the procedure if needed.

Drop-off: confirm the team can reach the client during the day, note any special instructions, and reassure an anxious owner. Leaving a pet for surgery is stressful — warmth here matters.

Double-check you have a reachable phone number for the day. If the doctor needs consent for something found during surgery, they must be able to reach the owner.
Always confirm a phone number where the owner can be reached DURING the procedure. If the team finds something needing additional consent mid-surgery, an unreachable owner is a serious problem.

blocks.front: Why must a CSR confirm a reachable phone number on a surgery day?
blocks.back: The medical team may need to reach the owner during the procedure for additional consent if something is found — an unreachable owner is a serious problem.

blocks.question: Before a surgery drop-off, which is essential to confirm?

options.feedback: Important, but not the most safety-critical item here.

options.text: Signed consent and a phone number where the owner is reachable during the procedure
options.feedback: Correct — consent and day-of reachability are essential for safe surgical care.

blocks.content:
Discharge is where the CSR ensures the pet goes home safely and the owner knows what to do.

A good surgical discharge:
• Coordinates pick-up timing with the medical team (the pet must be adequately recovered).
• Ensures the client leaves with written post-op instructions (activity restrictions, incision care, medications, warning signs to watch for).
• Schedules any recheck or suture-removal appointment before they leave.
• Reviews medications and confirms the client understands dosing.
• Provides clear guidance on who to call with post-op concerns.

Many post-op problems are prevented by clear discharge: a client who understands 'keep her quiet for 10 days and call us if the incision looks red or swollen' is set up for success.

Written post-op instructions are essential — owners are often distracted with relief at pickup and won't remember spoken directions. Send everything in writing and schedule the recheck before they leave.

blocks.front: Why provide written post-op discharge instructions?
blocks.back: Owners are distracted at pickup and forget spoken directions; written instructions on activity, incision care, meds, and warning signs prevent post-op problems.

blocks.prompt: At surgical discharge, the owner seems rushed and says 'just give me the basics.' What should you ensure before they leave?

options.text: Let them go with just the pet to save time
options.feedback: Skipping discharge instructions risks post-op complications.

options.text: Make sure they leave with written instructions, understand meds and activity restrictions, know warning signs, and have a recheck scheduled
options.feedback: Correct — the essentials of safe discharge must be covered even with a rushed owner.

options.text: Tell them to figure it out and call if there's a problem
options.feedback: Inadequate — clear discharge prevents problems in the first place.

blocks.content: You've covered the full surgical-day arc from a CSR's seat: scheduling, critical pre-op fasting instructions, estimates and consent, reachable contact info, and safe discharge with clear follow-up. You keep surgery days running safely and smoothly.
